data.title: Distillate Capital Q1 2025 Letter To Investors
data.description:
Letter Summary

Uncertainty is inherent to investing, but there are times like the present when it is more pronounced and investors’ fears of the unknown grip markets. The current moment also involves not one but a confluence of several major issues with uncertain answers: how will tariffs impact the economy and corporate profitability; will much-hyped AI technology live up to expectations; what is the path and sustainability of government spending; what can, and should the Fed do facing potential slowing in the economy with inflation as an accelerated risk? Academia and Wall Street have trained investors to focus on risk more than uncertainty and to quantify it through mathematical formulas built around price volatility. But uncertainty differs from risk in that it is unknowable and cannot be easily mathematized nor modeled and investors who are more trained in these risk measures become very uncomfortable when confronted with uncertainty. Our investment processes are designed with this in mind and instead of emphasizing price volatility and risk metrics based on historic price patterns, employ a combination of measures around leverage, cash flow stability, and valuation with the goal of both navigating through uncertain times and capitalizing on the opportunities they create. Stability and not being burdened with excessive financial leverage offer clear benefits in an adverse or uncertain period, but so too does valuation – something easily underestimated. There are many parallels to both the macroeconomic and market backdrop of exactly 25 years ago but what many investors forget is that while the overall market struggled significantly in the years that followed, there was enormous divergence in price performance depending on starting valuation and size as is shown in Figure 1. As we again enter uncertain times with economic fragility and an exogenous shock, questions surrounding a new technology and massive investments behind it, and an expensive and concentrated market, we are optimistic that the combination of a valuation discipline and quality measures may again prove highly differentiating.

Performance Summary

U.S. Fundamental Stability M Value (U.S. FSV): Amid a broader decline for the U.S. equities and a drop of -4.28% for the S&P 500, our U.S. FSV strategy outperformed by 3.3% with a more modest decline of -0.95% in the first quarter. The strategy lagged the Russell 1000 Value Index where the heavy energy, financial, and utility weights contributed to better performance. Annualized net of fee performance since inception remains solid at 0.9% ahead of the S&P 500 and 5.1% ahead of the Russell 1000 Value Index.

U.S. Small/Mid Cap Quality M Value (SMID QV): Our SMID QV strategy returned -9.30% in the first quarter on a total return basis. This outperformed the Russell 2000 Index by 0.18% and underperformed the Russell 2000 Value index by 1.55%. Annualized excess returns of our SMID QV strategy since inception and net of fees are G.0% and 5.8% ahead of those benchmarks, respectively.

International Fundamental Stability M Value (Intl. FSV): Amid a general outperformance of international stocks in the quarter, our International FSV strategy’s total return of 8.39% after fees exceeded the MSCI All Country Ex US ETF benchmark’s gain of G.20%. Annualized net of fee performance since inception is ahead of the benchmark by 0.14%.

U.S. Large Cap Value Long 130%/Short 30% (U.S. Value 130/30): Our 130/30 strategy returned 0.78% net of fees in the first quarter of 2025 vs. the S&P 500 Index’s return of -4.28%. This strategy is 3.4% ahead of the S&P 500 ETF on an annualized net of fee basis and above the Russell 1000 Value Index by 7.5% since inception.

Market Commentary:

After rising sharply in each of the prior two years and running well ahead of underlying free cash flows (See Figure 2), U.S. equities declined in the first quarter and fell sharply in the first few days of the second quarter following announced tariffs. The post quarter move through April 4th is shown by the blue dot in the chart below. Even after these declines, however, the market is still somewhat extended above underlying free cash flows and remains expensive in a historical context.

While tariffs are the major issue causing recent market declines, we see three main issues contributing to heightened levels of uncertainty and market weakness. Tariffs and macroeconomic policies are the first, new revelations that have dampened enthusiasm around AI are second, and the trajectory and sustainability of government spending is the final category. We will examine each.

Tariffs and Macroeconomic Uncertainty

One of the major uncertainties facing the market and weighing on stocks is the impact of tariffs and related macroeconomic policies. While the threat of impending tariffs had been an overhang for equities to some degree in the first quarter, President Trump’s announcement of significant and sweeping tariffs on foreign goods on April 2nd sent shock waves through global markets. The import duties announced then amount to an effective tariff of around 23%1 (See Figure 3 where the dashed red line represents the recently announced rate). This would mark a level not seen in a century and one that eclipses even the famous Smoot-Hawley tariffs that were made famous by the Great Depression. Additionally, the global economy is more integrated now than in the past and imports are much larger as a share of GDP, which is also highlighted in Figure 3.

When the recently announced effective tariff rate is combined with the import share of GDP, the resulting combination of the potential tariff share of GDP is enormous. This is clear in Figure 4 and highlights the scale of the potential shock from tariffs both in terms of inflation and the negative impact on economic activity. There are offsetting impacts to this simple combination, however, as some products will be substituted and some companies will not be able to pass through the costs to consumers and will absorb increased import costs through reduced profits. Those reduced profits, though, may lead to job losses and curtailed business investment which may result in broader economic weakness. On the other hand, some domestic producers may be able to raise prices to levels commensurate to the imported cost of competing goods and may see their profits increase while also stoking price pressures on domestic goods. Others may increase investment domestically to shore up production at home over time, but this also is highly uncertain.

A great deal will also depend on how trading partners respond. Another concern is the likelihood that companies will spend several years trying to recoup increased import costs through higher prices. This occurred during the supply chain induced inflation in the pandemic and was highly problematic. In turn there is the risk that an inflation shock from imports could seep into heightened inflation expectations if prices are seen rising for a more prolonged time and this could risk more general inflationary pressures that are difficult to combat. Adding to this already intricate complex mix is an unknown impact on consumption from potential wealth declines.

It is possible that tariffs could be reduced, either by way of negotiations or several brewing legal or legislative challenges. This is also uncertain and it is likely that the announcement itself has set wheels in motion that will result in a significant impact even if modifications are made. Overall, the macroeconomic shock introduced by tariffs is likely to be very large and inflationary, but the full consequences are extremely uncertain.

Another unfortunate aspect of the tariffs is that they are coming exactly at a moment when the economy was already softening along several dimensions. The Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index is a monthly survey that has historically provided a good measure of economic activity when the inventories component is subtracted from the orders component. Even before the tariff announcement, this measure, which leads GDP by G months in the chart (See Figure 5), was slowing sharply and pointing to a weaker overall economy.

Consistent with the broader economic slowdown indicated by the PMI survey, the National Federation of Independent Business survey of planned capital expenditures in the next three months was likewise signaling a slowdown in business investment (See Figure 6).

Measures of consumer health were also moderating. The share of consumers expecting higher versus lower incomes in six months has tracked core retail sales closely and was pointing to softer spending (See Figure 7).

Lastly, while inflation had been moderating after being very elevated, it remains above targeted levels and both consumer and business surveys were pointing to an acceleration, though some of this was likely due to expectations for tariffs.

In combination, these various measures of economic activity point to an economy that was already exhibiting signs of fragility before the tariffs were announced, and one that may be less durable to the tariff shock. Consequently, this makes the impact of the tariff hike and its reverberations even more uncertain with significant risk to the downside. The scale of the announced tariffs also puts us very much into uncharted territory and so it is extremely difficult to anticipate their full impact and all the complex follow-on impacts. The resulting uncertainty is therefore unlikely to dissipate soon and may remain an overhang investors must grapple with for some time.

AI Uncertainty

A second major uncertainty impacting equities is whether AI technology will deliver and ultimately generate profits on a level to justify the enormous investments being made. It is perhaps especially critical given the prices being given the stocks of those companies in the center of the boom and the degree to which they have provided stock market leadership. Should the bullishness turn out to be correct, there are questions about how those rewards will be distributed and which companies will see the greatest benefits. The prevailing wisdom had been that AI was an extremely capital-intensive endeavor and that only hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Oracle (ORCL) and a select few others could afford the enormous investment required to build AI data centers and these same companies were assumed to be the primary beneficiaries of the technology. It also followed that NVIDIA (NVDA) and other suppliers of the chips and equipment to build that computing capacity would be massive winners. Several recent developments, however, have challenged this prevailing view.

First, progress in AI is slowing even despite the enormous investments being made.1 Additionally, issues like hallucinations, or the delivery of false or incorrect information, have been reduced but appear endemic to the technology, which may limit applications and ultimate demand.2

Second, Chinese company DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) shocked the AI world with an AI model that cost a tiny fraction of what U.S. companies were spending. DeepSeek upended the thinking that capital intensity was a barrier to entry and that rewards would accrue to the largest incumbents. Microsoft and others countered this narrative with the idea that reduced costs will increase usage based on the example of increased coal demand from factories in industrial England that became more efficient users of coal. But this seems less applicable to AI demand today when much is already being offered for free and the revenue models are unclear.

Concerns about profitability are the third issue adding to uncertainty about the technology. While companies like OpenAI have been very successful in attracting users, revenues are still falling well short of costs3 and OpenAI’s key partner Microsoft, seems to be distancing itself from what was previously a very close partnership.4

Microsoft’s recent pull back from data center investments is the next development that has added to uncertainty.5 A former leader in the industry’s expansion, a pullback may indicate that the company is worried about overcapacity. Adding to the angst, Eddie Wu, CEO of Alibaba, the Chinese technology company heavily involved in AI, recently voiced concerns about the level of spending in the U.S. and raised the prospect that it was a bubble.6 This may be informed by the experience in China where large amounts of new data center capacity are reportedly going unused.7

As with the impact of tariffs, issues around the profitability and future path of AI technology are complex and unknown. Where there was previously unreserved optimism and an assumption that incumbents would only further their dominance, there is now creeping uncertainty.

Government Spending Uncertainty

A final area of uncertainty is the path and sustainability of government spending. While the Federal Government ran significant budget deficits around the pandemic to provide countercyclical spending, it has continued to spend even as the economy returned to full health and low unemployment. In fiscal years 2023 and 2024, the federal deficit amounted to G.2% and G.4% of GDP, respectively. Concerningly, increased costs of mandatory spending on social security, Medicare, Medicaid, interest expense, and several other categories now absorb that full amount of revenues such that all discretionary spending is being done with borrowed dollars (See Figure 8.) The trajectory of spending is also likely to worsen as tax cuts that were planned to expire for budgetary reasons are likely to be extended. The deficit and debt will worsen as this occurs and there will be little stimulus provided relative to current conditions since an extension would only maintain the level of tax rates today. Ultimately, these levels of deficit spending and debt accumulation are unsustainable and will require spending cuts, increased taxes, or a combination of the two. Exactly how or when this issue may intensify is unknown, but it is yet another uncertainty that is increasingly weighing on investment decisions.

Uncertainty M Valuation in the Past

The quote is well worn, but fitting -- Mark Twain -- “history doesn’t repeat itself, but it often rhymes”. The parallels between today’s economic and market backdrop and those of exactly 25 years ago are eerily similar. The late-1990’s also experienced a combination of a slowing economy and an economic shock, coupled with dissipating enthusiasm and uncertainty relating to an exciting new technology that propelled equities to new highs and extremely rich valuations and levels of concentration. While there are a number of key differences between then and now and the scale of market’s richness was higher then, there is much that looks alike. The gap between prices and fundamentals from Figure 2 looks similar. Both were driven by the excitement around a new and potentially transformative technology that spurred massive investment in supportive infrastructure (fiber then and data centers now). The enormous gains that accrued to the companies selling equipment for that infrastructure (Lucent, Cisco (CSCO) and others then vs. NVDIA, Broadcom (AVGO) and others today) is also similar. An economic recession officially began in March of 2001 but was exacerbated by the economic shock from the September 11 terror attack. The economic shock today comes from tariffs but is also occurring at a time that growth was showing signs of slowing.

While considering these remarkable similarities, it is easy to remember the market downturn that followed without adequately considering its nuances. Crucially, most of the market richness, where stock prices had driven the overall free cash flow yield to 2.2%, was due to the very biggest stocks, as the 10 largest stocks were trading at an extremely rich 1.1% free cash flow yield. The next biggest 90 stocks were also expensive, but less so, while the smallest 250 stocks were dramatically less expensive. Those differences in valuation in March of 2000 had an enormous impact on subsequent returns as the biggest and most expensive stocks suffered the most. While those stocks were down significantly two years later, the cheapest stocks were higher in absolute terms (See Figure 9), and brought the equal weighted index into positive territory as well.

The divergence in returns between the largest and most expensive stocks and the rest of the market did not stop quickly. While it took the capitalization-weighted S&P 500 nearly seven years to return to its March of 2000 levels, an equal weighted version of the benchmark was up over 80% in that same period (See Figure 10). Indeed, there was significant opportunity to be had below the surface.

Today, the market dynamic around valuation and concentration closely resembles conditions 25 years ago. Figure 11 shows a remarkably similar valuation composition where today’s largest stocks are trading more expensively than their smaller peers and the market averages. The chart includes our U.S. FSV strategy which is less expensive than either the equal weight or small stock group.

Back to Twain’s quote, we are optimistic that there may be good returns to be had today despite challenges just as there were in 2000. We also believe that it is extremely important that our strategy is achieving this valuation spread while filtering out stocks that have high leverage and unstable cash flow profiles, significantly differentiating it from the equal weighted index and the group of smaller stocks within the S&P 500.

U.S. Large Cap

The richness of the largest names in the market and the enormous resulting valuation dispersion has enabled our U.S. FSV strategy to achieve a record valuation spread over the S&P 500. While the S&P 500 has seen its free cash flow to enterprise value yield decline from around 4.5% to 3.7% in the seven years since we launched our strategy, the comparable metric for our U.S. FSV strategy has actually risen and ended the quarter at G.2% (See Figure 12). Notably, our strategy has outperformed the S&P 500 over this period despite its valuation becoming more attractive while the S&P 500 got much more expensive. At present, our free cash yield advantage is the highest it has ever been and the absolute free cash flow yield is at the same level as was the overall market during the depths of the crisis in 2009, an entry point for equities which in retrospect turned out to be very fortuitous.

We would also note that estimated free cash flows underpinning these valuation metrics are likely to change, as they are for the entire market, and so there is likely to be some movement in these valuation figures. By focusing on companies with more stable historical and estimated cash flows per share, however, the intention is that estimate revisions will be more stable for the stocks in our portfolio than they will be for the overall market. Companies that see significant deterioration in their normalized multi-year estimates or that see significant erosion in the stability of their cash flows will be sold from the portfolio as they would no longer meet the required criteria for inclusion and would not be considered among the names best equipped to weather turbulent times. We would also expect the portfolio to rebalance into newly cheap names that have low debt and good fundamental stability but that were sold off indiscriminately amid a broader period of panicked conditions. In this sense, in periods of volatility, the portfolio design both seeks to capitalize in the down market period by protecting capital, but also the eventual recovery by rotating into higher quality names that become newly cheap.

Small/Mid

We see much the same polarity in the opportunity set in the small and mid-cap space. As we have suggested in prior letters, the broader small stock indexes do not look that attractive in aggregate when properly assessed. There are two key reasons for this. First, the small stock indexes include a large portion of unprofitable companies that are dragging down the valuation of the overall group. Second, small companies have very high debt loads at present and this too is weighing on valuation when leverage is considered. Fortunately, there is enormous dispersion in the small cap space that allows us to navigate both issues. Figure 13 highlights our positioning across the valuation and leverage metrics while also showing the enormous range among the index’s constituents. Given the uncertainty facing the economy and market and potential headwinds, large debt burdens and a lack of profitability look to be an especially dangerous and combustible mix. While we worry that many small/mid stocks could struggle mightily, we do see significant opportunity for differentiation.

When these holdings are aggregated at the portfolio level, our Small/ Mid QV strategy has a substantially better valuation with much lower leverage than the small cap or large cap benchmarks (see Figure 14).

International

After a long stretch of underperformance, international stocks outperformed their domestic counterparts in the first quarter. Fifteen years ago, international stocks were more expensive than U.S. stocks measured using free cash flow. They also had more debt, a legacy of less stable cash flow generation, and faced slower growth. In combination, this made the U.S. market look like the clear winner from an investing perspective despite much commentary to the opposite and investor enthusiasm for foreign securities. Now, after a long period of underperformance, international stocks are finally less expensive than their U.S. counterparts and look appealing on a relative valuation basis.

The history of overall U.S. and international valuations as well as that of our own strategy are shown in Figure 15. International stocks do still have more debt and less stable cash flow profiles, but again those risk can be reduced by filtering out highly levered or fundamentally less stable stocks as our investment process does. Recognizing we risk being the proverbial broken record, we believe being selective is critical in improving the probability of success over the long term by gravitating to those stocks that are the least expensive that meet our quality criteria.

Final Word

Markets abhor uncertainty, yet it is at the very core of investing. Wall Street and academia are largely focused on measures of risk that are based on prior price moves and much less equipped to sit with the discomfort of uncertainty where probabilities are unknown. Worse, investors may become lulled into false confidence that risk measures provide them security, and then react rashly and sub-optimally when suddenly confronted with uncertainty and unexpected poor results. It is precisely this view that shaped our strategy’s design. We do not use short-term measures of risk utilizing stock price volatility that are prone to changing dramatically at exactly the moment you would ideally have them the most stable. Instead, we focus on more durable measures of quality and include valuation as a key component of the design that puts protecting capital in difficult times as our highest priority.

As noted earlier in this letter, valuation proved its efficacy in 2000 when the backdrop looked remarkably similar in many ways. Leverage and stability proved their value more recently in the highly challenging early months of the pandemic when conditions were extremely uncertain. In the first half of 2020, beta did a poor job of protecting capital with low beta names and strategies performing poorly and high beta stocks actually doing the best. By contrast, our measures of leverage and stability acted as we would have hoped, with the least indebted names and least fundamentally volatile stocks outperforming.

In periods of economic distress, we also think it is an important point of differentiation to be focusing on free cash flow per share rather than measures of earnings. We have written at length about why we believe free cash flows are a better valuation measure in an increasingly asset light economy, but one additional reason is that free cash flows tend to be more stable themselves as they are less subject to write downs and other accounting treatments that can make earnings-based measures highly volatile. This is also why Warren Buffett called net income “worse than useless.” The volatility of net income measures relative to free cash flows is seen in Figure 17. The chart shows both reported earnings based on GAAP accounting principles and operating earnings which are self-reported by the companies and unsurprisingly include highly favorable adjustments. In addition to the other reasons we prefer to use free cash flow, we believe its relative stability and lack of distortion from accounting issues is particularly important during challenging economic periods when net income figures are highly volatile and more likely to lead investors to mistakes in assessing valuation opportunities.

We do not know what the coming period will bring. But we don’t think anyone else does either. We are very much in uncertain times and much could change at any minute. Still, despite the potential challenges, the past may provide some guide given the remarkable similarities to the period 25 years ago. The lesson from that time was that the less expensive subsegments of the market can do quite well even if the overall market struggles amid uncertainty and a variety of headwinds. We also believe that focusing on the durability of cash flows and low debt levels are critical amid uncertain and challenging times. Like in car racing, victories come out of performance in the turns, not the straightaways, and we believe navigating the turns with free cash flow is the much better path.

Footnotes

  1. https://www.theinformation.com/articles/openai-shifts-strategy-as-rate-of-gpt-ai-improvements-slows?rc=wixrwf
  2. https://www.nature.com/articles/d4158G-025-000G8-5
  3. https://www.nytimes.com/2024/09/27/technology/openai-chatgpt-investors-funding.html
  4. Microsoft's Relationship With OpenAI Is Not Looking Good
  5. https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-04-03/microsoft-pulls-back-on-data-centers-from-chicago-to-jakarta
  6. https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-03-25/alibaba-s-tsai-warns-of-a-bubble-in-ai-datacenter-buildout
  7. https://www.technologyreview.com/2025/03/2G/1113802/china-ai-data-centers-unused/

data.title: Rise To The Top Of The World With Topicus.Com Inc.
data.description:
Please note all figures are in Euros, unless otherwise noted, as that is the company's reporting currency.

Introduction

Although the U.S. stock market has pulled back in recent weeks as a result of the implementation of tariffs, but may still appear expensive with an average P/E of ~26X. At the beginning of 2025 the U.S. market accounted for more than half of the global equity value. This indicates you can buy the U.S. for $50 or the rest of the world for $50. What is the better deal?

This concentration is among the highest in history and the U.S. only has less than 5% of the global population and accounts for only ~30% of global GDP. The U.S. has some of the greatest businesses on the planet with the widest moats like Amazon (AMZN), NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Visa (V), etc., which consistently grow earnings by double digits annually and arguably deserve higher valuations than your average company in other parts of the world.

The purpose of this article is not to get into whether the U.S. is overvalued or not, but to provide analysis on a stock with zero exposure to the U.S. market and compound earnings at levels comparable to the best U.S. stocks mentioned, and even trades at a slight discount.

Those who follow my work may recall that I recently wrote an article on Constellation Software (CSU:CA) titled Constellation Software: Head For The Stars With This Stellar Compounder. As a reminder CSU is a serial acquirer of Vertical Market Software businesses and has been in operation since 1995. CSU has been among the greatest serial acquirers over the past couple of decades with return on invested capital consistently being greater than 20% annually.

VMS solutions are designed specifically for a specific industry or industry segment. These businesses share many of the characteristics of horizontal software in that the revenues tend to be very "sticky" and recurring as there tends to be no more than three providers within the vertical in which they operate. Any attempt for a client to switch providers would likely cause too much costs in terms of business disruption that it wouldn't make sense to switch software. VMS businesses also tend to have closer relationships with their customers than horizontal as there are continual efforts to enhance, upgrade and customize products. VMS tends to be less scalable than horizontal as VMS software is only applicable to specific "niches." It is not like Microsoft Excel, which can be used in many different industries, so is more scalable.

One of the key risks noted in my last article is that CSU will need to continually find creative ways to deploy capital at high rates of return to justify their somewhat elevated valuation. As previously mentioned, VMS products are not highly scalable, there is only so much more market share and margin expansion they can squeeze.

As CSU has approached a 96 billion dollar valuation in CAD, it will become increasingly harder for them to continue to make acquisitions at the same pace and compound at the same level as the number of acquisition targets gets smaller. Especially when the targets produce only $5 to $10 Million in revenue.

It is just the law of large numbers!

CSU has, in fact, found ways to get around this and one of those ways is by merging their operating groups with software companies that they like and spinning them out as publicly traded operating groups. They have done this twice now with Lumine Group (LMN:CA) and, of course, Topicus.com Inc. (TSXV:TOI:CA).

Topicus actually began in the nineties as an operating group not long after CSU began operations. CSU bought Total Specific Solutions or TSS in 2013, which was a Dutch software company that was also in the business of acquiring Vertical Market Software businesses in the region. TSS was founded by the Stripe Order Family Office, who used the capital from selling their stake in a long held family business. CSU allowed the Stripe Order Family Office to retain a one-third interest. As part of the purchase, CSU promised they would provide capital and administrative services such as HR, accounting, legal, and tax support. CSU also kept the CEO Robin Van Peolje in place of TSS and is now the CEO of Topicus.

In 2020, CSU merged these entities and spun them out as Topicus. CSU still retains a ~30% stake in the entity, the original founding family owns ~40% of the entity while the remaining 30% is available to public shareholders.

The main reasons for doing these spin-offs were to use the equity of the TSS/Topicus operating group as a currency to make further acquisitions in Europe that they might not have been able to do as part of the conglomerate of CSU. It is also a dedicated vertical that employees can get compensated in because, as you will remember me discussing in my previous article, employees are required to put part of their bonus into the stock and buy on public markets. If the employees of TSS/Topicus were still part of the CSU conglomerate, the link between individual performance and that of the stock may become somewhat vague, whereas if TSS/Topicus is a publicly traded entity, employees have greater alignment of their incentives with the performance of the operating unit they are working in.

The law of large numbers is also less of an issue being a "stand alone" entity as the European market is not like the U.S. or Canada. The VMS market is much more fragmented as a result of language, cultural, payment systems and regulations that differ widely from nation to nation. There is much more opportunity for a VMS business to gain monopoly power in their specific vertical than would be the case in North America as local regulations often make it the standard that that software must be used in their market regardless of whether it is the best or not.

Buying a business with $5-$10 Million in revenues [CAD] is going to be par for the course with acquisitions in Europe given the fragmentation, so market sizes are smaller. This won't move the needle as much for CSU with a $96 Billion [CAD] market capitalization as it would with Topicus who only has a $9 Billion market capitalization.

Topicus had an IPO price of ~CAD63/share and now trades at CAD145/share so it has compounded at a rate of ~18% annually, although this is still short of its parent company that has compounded at 22% in that time, it has outperformed the U.S., Canada, and Europe major indices. Still, if Topicus could realize half the success of CSU, which was returning 30% in many of its earlier years, it would be considered a successful investment.

I am a believer that Topicus and CSU can continue to outperform, although CSU will likely outperform due to Topicus's success.

Investment Case

Organic Growth

Mark Leonard, who is President of CSU and sits on the board of Topicus sees having operating entities as publicly traded entities as a testing ground CSU can learn from and potentially reinvigorate their organic growth rates. Over the last few years, Topicus has had the slight edge in organic growth. Topicus has averaged 6% organic growth since 2019 while CSU has averaged 4%. As a reminder, organic growth is essentially growth from core operations, not from acquisitions. This tends to be lower at 1-5% annually for VMS companies relative to horizontal market companies which can grow at high single digits or even double digits as it is far more scalable.

Let's begin by looking at the revenue segments. Topicus reports its revenues similar to how CSU does. First, there is software licensing which is fees charged to clients for the use of the software products, often with multi-year arrangements. Second, there is Maintenance and other recurring revenue which is fees charged to clients for support on software products after delivery. This is by far the largest segment at 70-75% of revenue on an annual basis. Professional service revenue is fees charged to clients for customizing, implementing and integrating services with other software programs, training and professional consulting. This accounts for 24-28% of revenues and is the second-largest component of revenues. Lastly, there is Hardware Sales which is the resale of third-party hardware part of customer solutions and internally assembled hardware sales. This is the smallest segment, accounting for less than 1% of revenues.

Both CSU and Topicus have similar organic growth in Maintenance and recurring revenue at 5-8% annually. As I alluded to in my previous article, this is the best way to assess the health of the business as these are the recurring (SaaS) revenues and implies that they are growing the vertical with customer acquisition, if this growth was falling it could imply that they are either increasing pricing at the expense of greater churn, or more competition was coming online. Although licensing and professional service growth has been lumpy for both businesses, maintenance revenue growth is consistent.

What is eye-popping when comparing Topicus organic growth to that of CSU by the segments is that their organic growth in non-maintenance has averaged much higher rates over the last five years.

It's difficult to say why Hardware has grown at a much higher rate for Topicus than CSU, but it doesn't move the needle a lot for either business as it is such a small component of revenue. Topicus, on the other hand, has been realizing higher growth from licensing fees and professional services revenue. This just goes to show how much more sticky VMS software is in Europe versus North America. The businesses Topicus owns in Europe tend to be more monopoly like within their vertical so they can raise prices and charge more for professional services because there are less options for customers to go with competitors, and if there were, the switching costs would be too high.

On the other hand, VMS businesses are even less scalable than in North America. A good example of this is library management software Topicus owns in Spain. It is unlikely you could take that software to say Poland as the language is different and there is potentially regulatory protection for another similar software in Poland. It would make more sense for Topicus to just acquire the Polish software maker.

VMS Market Dynamics

Now that we have established lower price sensitivity in Europe relative to North America. We will discuss competition. Not so much client competition but competition for acquisition targets.

The concern for CSU has been about when their stellar growth stalls because they either run out of acquisition targets or they have to accept a lower hurdle rate on their acquisitions.

According to the 2025 Europe Vertical Market Software Market Report, Europe commands as much as 30% of the global VMS market. The market size was estimated to be $36 Billion in USD as of 2023 (~$41 Billion in Euro at an FX rate of 1.14). TOI did $1.3 Billion Euro in revenue in F2024 so it has penetrated 3% of the market. The report also has the VMS market growing at a double-digit pace in Europe at least for the next five years for many of the same reasons that it is growing globally at these rates. I cited these reasons in my CSU article, but they are mainly because of increased usage of cloud computing, adoption of the Internet of Things, increased remote work and mobility solutions, supply chain optimization, and data-based decision-making. The entities expected to increase their usage of VMS are finance and banking, legal, healthcare and IT.

There are plenty of acquisition targets available with only having such a small portion of the VMS market. The runway for growth is bolstered by the fact that according to Statistica there is 2,548 private equity firms in all of Europe which would be the most formidable competition for deals, almost half of which are based in the U.K. For reference, according to IBIS World there is 8,450 in the U.S. The U.S. has more than three times the amount of PE firms despite its population being less than half the size of Europe. In general, businesses that don't do more than $10 Million in revenue are less attractive for PE firms, which is within Topicus's parameters.

Where CSU has been in an advantageous position relative to PE firms in its lifetime is that PE firms are more short term focused. PE firms tend to look for ways to either cut costs often via reducing staff or increase license and professional service fees at the expense of customer acquisition and may even increase churn. This is because PE firms tend to have a shorter holding period where they are just trying to extract value in less than 10 years, whereas CSU has only sold one company in its history.

Even if CSU is not willing to pay as high a price, owners have opted to sell to them, especially when it's a founder, as they have trusted CSU will advance the vision of ownership and maintain the level of customer service post-acquisition. Topicus operates under the same framework as CSU in this regard.

It is still possible that Topicus competes with large corporations like SAP SE (SAP) and Siemens AG (OTCPK:SIEGY) as they may choose to acquire rather than outsource for easier collaboration to tailor to their needs. These companies have the resources to do so.

Capital Deployment

Since the IPO in 2020, Topicus has completed 227 Million Euro worth of acquisitions. As per CSU's 2024 MD&A, Topicus completed 414 Million Euro of acquisitions in Q1 2025, which is more than they have deployed in the time since their IPO.

These transactions are summarized as follows:

  • On January 31, 2025, the Company purchased 8,300,029 shares in Asseco Poland S.A. (“Asseco”) representing approximately 9.99% of the issued shares in Asseco. The shares were acquired at a price of 85 PLN per share. Asseco offers comprehensive, proprietary IT solutions for all sectors of the economy.
  • On February 4, 2025, the Company entered into a binding agreement in respect of the acquisition of 12,318,863 treasury shares of Asseco. These shares represent 14.84% of Asseco’s share capital and will be purchased at a price of PLN 85 per share. The completion of the acquisition of the treasury shares remains subject to obtaining relevant regulatory and antitrust approvals.

Asseco (OTCPK:ASOZF) is the largest IT company in Poland and actually trades on their major exchange. Poland is not a market CSU has tons of experience in and few connections. Topicus has taken a ~9% interest in this entity to utilize their connections to build their database for acquisition targets. Topicus is in exchange providing them resources in business strategy, legal, and HR.

The end result is we should expect greater revenue growth in the next couple of years relative to the previous three years as a result of this partnership.

Valuation

There are very few publicly traded comparables outside of CSU and Lumine. The only other one that is not part of the CSU ecosystem is Roper Technologies (ROP). I prefer to look at these companies on an EV/EBITDA basis or free cash flow basis as they have significant amortization of intangible assets which makes their earnings power look worse than it really is.

Roper makes acquisitions in the multi-billion dollar range, whereas CSU and Topicus make acquisitions in the tens or at most hundred million dollar range. Roper will accept lower hurdle rates with the hopes of juicing organic growth to be more competitive in deals. Roper is a little bit more of a dividend play as its growth has been slower than CSU and Topicus, only growing FCF at a ~17% CAGR over the last three years compared to ~24% for the other two. Roper still surprisingly trades at a higher valuation.

This may be in part because Topicus trades on the TSX Venture, so it will inevitably be more thinly traded as many institutional funds will be restricted from investing in it due to its lower liquidity. Its FCF growth has been comparable to CSU, however yet, it trades at the lower valuation as well.

I conduct a reverse DCF model to determine what the FCF growth rate/ROIC will need to be over the next decade to determine if we are getting a good price at 145 Euro/share. Organic growth provides strong guidance for the terminal growth rate, as that will likely be their growth rate when they can no longer grow by acquisition. I have elected to use an organic growth rate of 5% which is the 6-year average, an FCF margin of 14%, and WACC of 10%. The table below shows that Revenue/FCF growth would have to be ~21% annually over the next decade to justify this valuation. The numbers below are in millions, except per share data.

The reverse DCF implies that FCF will have to grow at ~21% on an annual basis over the next decade for Topicus to be fairly valued. CSU managed to compound FCF in many of its early years at these rates and does consistently 30 years later. Topicus has 30 years of learning experience from CSU that they can rely on and with operating in a very fragmented market with even less competition than CSU. I see 20%+ growth on a YoY basis for the next decade as very plausible.

In fact, they have compounded FCF at 24% since 2021, which is higher than many American "pure software" firms which on a PEG basis actually makes Topicus cheaper.

Conclusion

As a result of the highly fragmented market in Europe and lack of competition from PE firms for acquisitions in Europe, and the 30-year operational know how of CSU, I believe that 20%+ FCF growth with ROICs over 20% for the foreseeable future is likely for Topicus. The monopoly power of VMS businesses in Europe also makes Topicus a high terminal growth business where even if it runs out of acquisition targets it will have the ability to increase licensing fees and professional services more so than CSU. Therefore, I believe the analyst price targets of 150-160 CAD per share are justified.

I see this as a double-digit compounder for many years.

In order to determine whether Topicus is a better investment than CSU depends on your global macroeconomic outlook. CSU has more global exposure with 50% of revenues from the U.S. 30% from Europe, and 10% from other countries as of 2023 YE, whereas Topicus is strictly Europe. So you get more geographic exposure with CSU at a slightly higher multiple. CSU does own 30% of Topicus so as a CSU shareholder, you benefit from the growth of Topicus anyway.

One of the risks for both of these entities is how AI will impact the market. The bear case is AI just writes software instead of people driving down the cost and making it easier for clients to switch. The business disruption risk may stop them from switching too. I still believe we are years away from AI writing all software. CSU and Topicus could also benefit as it might expand the available targets if AI makes it cheaper to write software and therefore encourages more entrants.

Another risk is that Topicus trades on the TSX Venture Exchange. This is an exchange for small-cap companies whose assets are too small to be listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange. Topicus has an average daily trading volume of 26,000. For perspective, CSU's volume is almost double that, so it does have liquidity risk. Trading at such low volumes will inevitably exclude from the mandate of many global asset managers, which will limit multiple expansion.

It is also unlikely we see share buybacks as CSU hasn't done any in their history. This is because employees are required to purchase shares as part of their bonus, and they don't want to artificially inflate the price. Currently, the insider ownership of Topicus is 2%, so there is still some shareholder alignment, but unlike CSU which has insider ownership at ~7%. It is still possible that they pay a dividend at some point as CSU did.

I recommend Topicus as a Strong Buy!

data.title: BYD Company: A Better Bet Than Tesla
data.description:
BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY) benefited from surging delivery volumes in March and in the first-quarter as the company's EV products remain highly popular, both in China and abroad. The world’s biggest electric vehicle, or EV, company saw a rebound in deliveries in March, which is when EV sales start to normalize after the Chinese Lunar New Year holidays in January and February. Further, BYD just days ago released its expectations for first-quarter earnings which showed a massive upswing in profitability. Overseas exports are also surging, making BYD one of the most attractive EV plays in the Chinese market, especially when compared against a group of start-up companies in China. With Tesla (TSLA) also recently reporting weaker delivery figures for Q1, I believe BYD is a potentially more promising investment than Tesla.

Previous rating

I rated shares of BYD a strong buy in my last work on the Chinese EV giant, mainly because the electric vehicle company overtook Tesla (TSLA) in the fourth-quarter in terms of global deliveries. Additionally, BYD is priced at an extremely attractive valuation based off of earnings and is leading the industry group in terms of gross margins. BYD also recently announced that it is going to roll out its advanced driver assistance program to its entire fleet (including its low-cost electric vehicles) which is set to make BYD vehicles even more attractive to prospective buyers and could lead to an acceleration of its delivery figures.

Strong delivery rebound in March, BYD still leads the competition in terms of gross margins

BYD’s deliveries rebounded hard in the month of March 2025: the electric vehicle, which is known around the world for low-cost EVs such as the BYD Seagull or the BYD Seal, sold 377,420 new energy vehicles in the previous month, which includes both battery-powered and plug-in hybrid EVs, showing 24.8% growth year-over-year.

Battery-powered electric vehicle, or BEV, sales totaled 166,109 units, showing 18.7% Y/Y growth, while the EV company sold 205,310 plug-in hybrids, or PHEVs, showing 27.0% Y/Y growth. Both BEVs and plug-in hybrids sales bounced upward in March, showing a healthy recovery from suppressed January and February delivery levels due to the Chinese New Year holidays.

Additionally, BYD sold 72,723 vehicles in overseas markets in March, showing 89.2% Y/Y growth. BYD overseas figures includes electric vehicles that are manufactured in China, but shipped to other markets. March overseas deliveries were the highest number ever for the EV company in March 2025.

In terms of deliveries, BYD did about as well as Chinese electric-vehicle start-ups in March. The industry group I am using below includes Li Auto (LI), NIO (NIO) and XPeng (XPEV). While all EV manufacturers in the industry group saw a significant delivery upswing, the fastest growth in March was seen by XPeng which benefited from 267.9% year-over-year growth in March 2025 due to the success of its low-cost brand Mona.

Tesla, on the other hand, reported a 13% decline in vehicle deliveries in Q1 '25, which compares against a delivery growth rate for BYD of 38.7% year-over-year (only using comparable battery-powered electric vehicles). Tesla had by far the weakest delivery results of any major EV maker in the first-quarter.

BYD, now the world’s biggest electric vehicle company, submitted its expectations for its first-quarter earnings days ago: BYD sees 8.5-10.0B Chinese Yuan ($1.2-1.4B) in earnings for Q1. Based off of BYD’s Q1 ’25 earnings guidance, profits are expected to surge between 86% and 119% on a year-over-year basis. This growth is mainly due to surging shipping volumes in both China and in export markets.

What makes BYD an especially interesting EV play, in my opinion, and a potentially better EV play than Tesla, is that the electric vehicle company is already widely profitable and achieving a higher gross margin than Tesla. In fact, most EV start-ups are achieving significantly lower gross margins than BYD and only Li Auto, with a gross margin of 20.3%, beat BYD. The Chinese EV maker reported a gross margin of 17.0% compared to Tesla's 16.3% margin.

BYD is a better deal than Tesla

BYD is valued at a price-to-revenue ratio of 0.86X -- based off of FY 2026 revenues -- which makes the electric vehicle company the cheapest large-cap EV play in the industry group. BYD is as cheap as it is because many U.S. investors are very hesitant to invest in Chinese EV companies for a number of reasons, such as ownership concerns, governance risks and the fact that the Chinese government inserted itself into the affairs of its largest technology companies in FY 2020.

From a valuation point of view, however, I believe BYD is the best large-cap bet on the EV market that investors can make because:

  1. The firm is the largest by delivery volume,
  2. It has massive delivery momentum in China and in export markets, and
  3. BYD is already profitable and is situated at the top of the industry group in terms of gross margins.

In my last work on BYD, I said that the Chinese EV firm could more than double its valuation based off of a fair value P/S ratio of ~2.0X which I still believe is very much achievable given the trajectory we are seeing in BEV and PHEV deliveries. A 2.0X P/S ratio gives BYD a fair value in the neighborhood of $86 per-share.

Tesla, which is America’s largest EV-focused automaker, is trading at a massively higher price-to-revenue ratio of 6.3X, while Rivian Automotive (RIVN) is valued at a forward P/S ratio of 1.7X. Chinese EV start-ups like NIO, Li Auto and XPeng are also significantly cheaper due to continual revenue and delivery ramps and profitability challenges (with the exception of Li Auto which is already profitable). The industry group average P/S ratio is about ~2.0X which why I believe BYD has massive upside revaluation potential going forward.

Risks with BYD

An escalation of the tariff war between the U.S., Europe and China could have dire consequences for BYD, although the company is not selling EVs directly in the U.S. Higher tariffs could lead to a shrinking of global trade which potentially could hurt expansion-driven BYD exactly at a time when it is ramping up its exports into other countries. In addition, U.S. investors are right about citing higher governance and transparency risks when it comes to investments in Chinese EV companies, so they may continue to stay on the sidelines and avoiding BYD as an investment.

Final thoughts

BYD is focused on ramping up deliveries for both its battery-powered and plug-in hybrids, in both China and in export markets. The EV firm has seen a steep increase in sales in both core product groups in March and in Q1 '25, and just submitted a solid earnings projection for the first quarter. Because BYD delivers more than 1 million new energy vehicles each quarter, the Chinese EV firm has such a big scale that it is already profitable… which sets it apart from other electric vehicle company, especially in the Chinese industry group which includes mostly start-ups. I like that BYD is trading at a very attractive valuation based off of revenue and I see investment advantages for the EV company given that it has a leading gross margin profile and the biggest delivery volume.

data.title: 10-Year Treasury Yield Snaps Back To February's 4.5%, Yield Curve Re-Un-Inverts, Mortgage Rates Back At 7%
data.description:
The aggressive bash-down by the White House of long-term Treasury yields and the dollar since January worked. Until it didn’t.

The 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.49% on Friday, back where it had been on February 20. It has snapped back by 50 basis points from the recent low on April 3 of 3.99% after a hard plunge.

By comparison: Starting in mid-September, the 10-year Treasury yield surged by 116 basis points to 4.79% on January 10, 2025, and there was no talk of an impending catastrophe. But now that the 10-year yield has risen by only 50 basis points to only 4.49%, and is once again barely above the effective federal funds rate (blue in the chart below), the media is generating piles of scary headlines about an impending catastrophe?

Starting in January, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent began actively bashing down long-term Treasury Yields and the dollar. Lower long-term yields would translate into lower funding costs for the US economy. And a weaker dollar would boost the economy by favoring exports (a positive in GDP) over imports (a negative in GDP). This was the explicit two-pronged effort by the White House to give the economy a boost. And the bash-down worked. Until it didn’t.

The bash-down caused the 10-year Treasury yield to careen down 80 basis points, from its recent high on January 10 of 4.79% to 3.99% on April 3. That was a lot very fast.

But there was an issue with this bash-down of the yield: Inflation is sticking around, and may be accelerating further, and the Fed is worrying about higher inflation rates going forward based on the tariffs, and is worrying that this inflation might become “persistent.” Future inflation rates might be in the 3% range or higher. The long-term Treasury market fears out-of-whack inflation more than anything.

But amid the bash-down, the 10-year Treasury yield went from 4.79% in January, which was already somewhat unappetizing in this inflationary environment, to 3.99% on April 3, which was outright gross in this inflationary environment. And investors lost interest at these low yields. Yields had to shoot up to where the buyers were, and so the 10-year yield snapped back after having been push down too far, and on Friday closed at 4.49%.

That higher yield brought out demand, including foreign demand

At the 10-year Treasury auction on Wednesday, there was very strong demand from indirect bidders, which include foreign buyers, including foreign central banks. The auction was described as “stellar.” The $39 billion in notes were sold at a yield of 4.435%.

Then, on Thursday, there was the “stellar” 30-year Treasury auction, where $22 billion in 30-year bonds were sold at a yield of 4.813%, amid very strong demand from indirect bidders which include foreign investors.

So the White House bash-down of the 10-year Treasury yield worked, until it didn’t, as the market ran out of investors that want to commit funds for 10-years at 4%. But at the current yields, investors scrambled to buy at the auctions.

The longer view going back to 2022 shows just how funny the fretting in the media and by the Wall Street crybabies is:

The 30-year Treasury yield closed at 4.87% on Friday, back where it had been on January 31, and below the near-5% rate on January 10, and well below the 5%-plus range where it had been in October 2023.

It has traded in a fairly narrow range since mid-2023, and the recent snap-back from the drop, when seen over this time frame, wasn’t much to write home about.

Yield curve re-un-inverted, still with a sag in the middle

The chart below shows the yield curve of Treasury yields across the maturity spectrum, from 1 month to 30 years, on three key dates:

  • Gold: January 10, 2025, just before the Fed officially pivoted to wait-and-see.
  • Red: April 11, 2025.
  • Blue: September 16, 2024, just before the Fed’s rate cuts started.

Rate cuts are on ice, and so short-term yields haven’t moved much and remain glued to the EFFR.

Longer-term yields have snapped back from the recent lows, but are still lower than on January 10.

As a result, yields of 7 years and longer are now once again higher than short-term yields, and that part of the yield curve has re-un-inverted. But there is still this sag in the middle, though it is shallower than it was two weeks ago.

In a Treasury market that doesn’t get aggressively bashed down by the White House, longer-term yields in this inflationary environment should be higher than they’re now. A 10-year yield of about 5% and a 30-year yield north of that would look about right, in my view.

Mortgage rates are back over 7%

The 30-year fixed mortgage rate roughly tracks the 10-year Treasury yield, but is higher, and that spread between the two varies.

Following the White House interest rate bash-down, mortgage rates fell with the 10-year yield, and now they too snapped back.

The average 30-year fixed mortgage rate on Friday rose to 7.07%, according to the daily measure by Mortgage News Daily. In the era before QE started, 7% mortgage rates were at the low end. In today’s inflationary environment, 7%-plus mortgage rates are somewhere close to reasonable.

The dollar bash-down

The White House bash-down of the dollar was also very effective, but unlike the 10-year Treasury yield, the dollar hasn’t snapped back yet. Though it will, sooner or later.

The dollar remains in its 3-year trading range and is higher than it was before that trading range. I had a little bit of fun on Friday with the catastrophist handwringing in the media over the dollar: OMG the Dollar Is Collapsing, or Whatever.

The dollar index (DXY), representing a basket of six currencies dominated by the euro and yen, dropped from 110 in January to 99.78 on Friday. But that drop was a lot smaller than the drop in 2022, and a lot smaller than prior drops, after which the dollar always bounced back. There were many years when the DXY was below 80. And at the current level, the dollar is still relatively high compared to where it had been in the prior 50 years. My article also includes a chart going back to the 1970s.

Original Post

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Tariff War Unlocks Dirt-Cheap Compelling Entry Points, Strong Buy Here
data.description:
TSM Is Even More Attractively Valued After The Recent Meltdown, Offering Interested Investors With The Rare/ Cheap Buying Opportunity

We previously covered Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) in January 2025, discussing why it remained a long-term Buy due to its leading N3/ N5 market share and the hyperscalers' promising AI/ data center demand growth over the next few years.

Combined with the double beat FQ4 2024 performance, strong FQ1 2025 guidance, and rich FY 2025 capex numbers, with it signaling a new investment/ growth cycle during the foundry's strategic global expansion, we had believed that it remained well positioned for long-term growth, supported by government subsidies.

TSM 1Y Stock Price

Since our recommended Buy rating between the 50-day moving averages of $197s or the 100-day moving averages of $189s, TSM has unfortunately breached even the 200-day moving averages of $173s to return most of its H2 2024 and Q1 2025 gains.

Much of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the bursting AI bubble by mid-February 2025, worsened by the intensified tariff war and the mixed macroeconomic environment, with it already triggering the deep market wide sell off by -21.4% between the 52-week heights and the recent bottom.

Before the recent tariff exemption on smartphones, computers, and other electronics announced on April 12, 2025, we can understand why TSM has pulled back as it has, with a similar correction observed in its semiconductor and foundry peers in varying degrees.

While the new US administration has previously exempted the import of semiconductor chips from the ongoing tariff war, it is apparent that there may be great impacts on the semiconductor sector ahead, since semiconductors are rarely used in its raw form.

With China facing a steep 145% import tariffs, Japan 24%, Germany 20%, India 26%, and South Korea 25% prior to the recent 90 days pause, we believe that finished electronic products including smart devices and data center components may get a lot more expensive moving forward, given that these countries comprise 48.5% of the global manufacturing output.

This is especially since the US "imported some $521 billion of machinery, $478 billion of electronics and $386 billion of vehicles last year, according to a Bernstein Research analysis," with the higher costs on finished products likely to eventually trigger an impacted demand on semiconductor chips down the line.

Well then, recent exemptions imply that the market pessimism surrounding semiconductor related companies may have been overly done, with it implying that the worst of the headwind may already be behind us. Naturally, this is assuming that China also lifts its 125% tariff import.

For now, while Alphabet (GOOG) had "reaffirmed the company pledge to invest about $75B to build out data center infrastructure," it remains to be seen if other hyperscalers may continue doing so ahead as well.

This is especially since the higher recessionary risks at 60% (up from the prior estimates of 40%) may trigger the hyperscalers' lowered capex guidance, depending on how things develop in the upcoming FQ1'25 earnings season in April 2025.

As a result, interested investors may want to tune into the upcoming earnings calls to better ascertain the impact of the ongoing tariff war, with it implying further volatility in the intermediate term.

On the other hand, numerous AI SaaS companies have continued to report the growing adoption of generative/Agentic AI across enterprises/ federal end markets, including Palantir (PLTR) and Salesforce (CRM), with it likely to drive the long-term demand for semiconductor chips, no matter the temporal macro/ geopolitical headwinds.

At the same time, TSM has already reported the promising preliminary FQ1 2025 sales growth by +41.6% YoY, while building upon the rich growth observed a quarter ago at +38.8% YoY and a year ago at +16.5% YoY, with it underscoring the durability of data center capex boom.

If anything, readers must note the tailwinds arising from the $500B Stargate project in the US, with the first data center set to be operational from H2 2026 onward, as numerous data center REITs continue to report their sold-out positions even for buildings under construction, including Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR).

While some of TSM's robust FQ1 2025 numbers may be attributed to the pulled forward sales cadence arising from "electronics makers had built up inventories in U.S. warehouses, preparing for possible trade disruptions," we are not overly concerned indeed, since it only proves the indispensable nature of the semiconductor industry no matter the ongoing macro/ geopolitical headwinds.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

The same optimism surrounding the semiconductor industry has also been observed in the promising consensus forward estimates, with TSM expected to chart an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +28.8%/ +18.8% through FY 2027, respectively.

This is compared to the original estimates of +8.7%/ +15.1%, the 5Y historical growth at +22%/ +27.7%, and the 10Y growth at +14.3%/ +16.1%, respectively, underscoring why the recent stock price correction has been unwarranted.

TSM Valuations

These reasons are also why we believe that TSM appears extremely cheap at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 17.57x and FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.59x, compared to the 1Y mean of 19.52x/ 0.48x, 5Y mean of 22.50x/ 0.81x, and 10Y mean of 17.50x/ 1.08x, respectively.

The same conclusion may also be observed after comparing to its direct foundry peers, including Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) at the historical 5Y PEG non-GAAP ratio mean of 2.38x (prior to the memory correction in 2023) and Intel (INTC) at 5Y mean of 0.82x (prior to the PC demand destruction in 2022).

When comparing the Taiwanese foundry to the two other AI godfathers, including ASML Holding (ASML) at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio mean of 1.35x and Nvidia (NVDA) at 0.68x, it goes without saying that there is an excellent margin of safety for those looking to add the market leading foundry with a global share of 67% as of Q4 2024 (+6 points YoY/ +15 from 2019 levels of 52%).

So, Is TSM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

TSM 5Y Stock Price

For now, TSM appears to have bounced after the worst of the market meltdown, as the bulls defend the $141s floor by early April 2025.

The selloff has directly triggered the narrower gap to our fair value estimates of $158.40, based on the FY 2024 adj EPS of $7.04 (+35.9% YoY) and the 5Y P/E mean valuations of 22.50x.

It has also contributed to the richer upside potential of +76% to our long-term price target of $266.10, based on the consensus raised FY 2027 adj EPS from $11.66 to $11.83, with it presenting a rare buying opportunity during the ongoing cloud super cycle.

While there may be growth headwinds in 2025, attributed to the ongoing tariff war as retailer "pulls the plug on China orders" and the tougher YoY comparisons, we believe that the worst of the market pessimism may already be baked in with a potential floor at $141 also materializing.

As a result of the rich double digits capital appreciation prospect arising from the ongoing market wide volatility and the improved margin of safety arising from the -33.2% stock price decline from the 52-week heights of $226.40, we are upgrading the TSM stock as a Strong Buy here.

While we concur that the "trade-related uncertainty is far from over," it goes without saying that the wider stock market (TSM included) is likely to rally back to previous heights, upon the resolution of the tariff war, and the lifting of market uncertainties, as similarly observed by +6.9% recovery from the April 08, 2025 bottom.

This reason is also why investors should simply buy “wonderful companies at a fair price” and holding through the massive market volatilities over a long period of time - in which “time in the market beats timing the market.”

Do not miss this rare dollar cost averaging opportunity.

On the other hand, readers must continue to note the risks related to TSM's investment thesis, attributed to the developing tariff war, as the new US administration "is looking at semiconductors and the electronics supply chain as part of upcoming national security tariff investigations."

This implies that the recent exemption may be temporary, with the rapidly changing policy environment likely to trigger a similarly volatile stock price movement - one that is only suitable for those with a higher risk tolerance and longer investing trajectory.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Apple Leads Q1 Global Smartphone Sales
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The iPhone had the largest market share in Q1, followed by Samsung. (0:15) Pfizer abandons weight-loss pill. (2:50) China halting rare earth exports. (3:36)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhone took the top spot in global smartphone sales for Q1 2025, with a 19% share of the market, according to Counterpoint Research.

Sales in the U.S., Europe and China were either flat or declining, but Apple saw double-digit growth in Japan, India, Middle East and Africa and Southeast Asia.

The iPhone saw a +4% year-over-year rise.

South Korea's Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) came in second with 18% market share. The company had a slow start due to the late launch of the S25 series, but sales showed a resurgence after the launch of the flagship S25 and new A-series devices.

Senior Research Analyst Ankit Malhotra said: "Sales in January were particularly strong, with a subsidy-led demand boost in China. The momentum continued with major launches like Samsung’s S25 and iPhone 16e, but it turned quickly as economic uncertainties and trade war risks started mounting, especially towards the end of the quarter."

Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) (OTCPK:XIACY) took the third spot with a 14% share. The company was helped by expansion into new markets and significant growth in the domestic market, where the brand is seeing "a bigger and more ‘premium’ brand presence thanks to its successful foray into electric vehicles."

The fastest-growing top five brand, vivo, gained rose to fourth place, helped by high exposure to the China market and expansion into emerging markets. Meanwhile, OPPO rounded off the top five, and saw sales growth in India, Latin America and Europe.

Overall, the global smartphone market grew 3% year-over-year in the first quarter of 2025, according to preliminary results from Counterpoint.

Among active stocks, Crocs (CROX) warned its employees to closely manage expenses due to the higher supply chain costs the company anticipates due to the volatility around the trade war between the U.S. and other nations. Crocs produces about half of its products in Vietnam, which is a target for higher tariff rates.

In a company memo spotted by Bloomberg, CEO Andrew Rees told staff to remain prudent for the remainder of the year and be thoughtful about travel and which initiatives to prioritize. In addition, the board of directors' compensation committee has agreed to establish more realistic targets for the footwear maker's internal incentives plan.

Pfizer (PFE) said it would no longer develop its oral weight loss therapy, danuglipron, following a liver injury found in a participant who took part in a study for a once-daily version of the drug.

Citing data from a dose-optimization study for once-daily danuglipron, Pfizer said a single asymptomatic participant encountered a “potential drug-induced liver injury” after receiving the GLP-1 receptor agonist.

Bank of America has reiterated its top semiconductor stocks to own, a list that includes Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semi (TSM), KLA (KLAC) and Lam Research (LRCX).

Analysts said: “Even though electronics tariffs have been delayed and not necessarily canceled, we view the 1-2 month pause as a net positive as it allows for debate/discussion on this critical end-market that contributes ~50% of all chip demand.”

In other news of note, China has suspended rare earth exports in retaliation to recent U.S. tariff hikes. The New York Times says the Chinese government is drafting a new regulatory system for exports, and, while the policies are being framed, the shipments of the magnets, essential for the manufacturing of almost everything ranging from cars to missiles, have been halted at many Chinese ports.

On April 4, China imposed new restrictions on the export of six heavy rare earth elements -- metals that are refined exclusively in China -- as well as rare earth magnets.

Beijing produces around 90% of the world's rare earths, a group of 17 elements used across the defense, electric vehicle, energy and electronics industries.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman equity strategist David Kostin says; “The performance gap between high- and low-liquidity stocks has remained surprisingly stable despite recent market turbulence and worsening liquidity conditions,”

Typically, in times of market stress, highly liquid stocks are among the first to be sold as investors look to reduce risk quickly. This dynamic often causes these stocks to underperform during broad market drawdowns. But Kostin noted that the current environment is proving to be an exception. The high liquidity basket tracked by Goldman has demonstrated resilience, thanks in part to the strong fundamental characteristics of its constituents.

Goldman recently rebalanced two custom equity baskets, each with 50 stocks from the Russell 1000.

Among the most liquid stocks are the Magnificent 7, Visa, (V), AMD (AMD) and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A). Among the most illiquid TFS Financial (TFSL), U-Haul (UHAL), First Hawaiian (FHB), Seaboard (SEB) and TripAdvisor (TRIP).

Check out all 100 names.
